topic: Concurrency - Once Cell and Lazy Init

turns.content: I thought I could just use a normal variable to store my app's config data since it only gets set once at startup, right? So if someone else runs the code in another thread, they'll see the same value without any extra work.

That assumption is actually a common trap. A simple variable doesn't guarantee that initialization happens exactly once across threads; concurrent access can lead to race conditions where multiple threads write the data simultaneously or read before it's fully ready. You need something specifically designed for this, like std::sync::OnceLock which uses internal locking to ensure the value is initialized only the first time it's accessed, and then subsequent accesses are just a simple read without any overhead.
Oh, I see. So once_cell::sync::OnceCell works similarly but maybe with different guarantees? And what about Lazy<T> from std::sync or similar crates? Are they all the same thing conceptually?

std::sync::OnceLock is part of the standard library and provides a basic mechanism for lazy initialization, while once_cell::sync::OnceCell offers an alternative that's often more ergonomic in certain contexts. Both ensure that the value is initialized exactly once even when accessed from multiple threads. Lazy<T> usually refers to a generic pattern or wrapper around such mechanisms, but the core concept remains the same: delaying computation until the first access.
Got it. So if I'm initializing a huge dataset like a map of all my user profiles, using these types saves me from having to check a flag and do a bunch of conditional logic before every use?

Exactly. Think of it like this: imagine you're building a house where the foundation is laid only once at the beginning. If you keep checking whether the foundation exists before every step, you waste time and energy. Once the foundation (the initialized data) is there, all your workers (threads) can just walk on top of it without needing to check again or rebuild anything.

That analogy makes perfect sense! So I don't need any if-flags or manual locking logic anymore? I can just wrap my data in std::sync::OnceLock and let the runtime handle the initialization ordering automatically?

Precisely. By wrapping your data in std::sync::OnceLock, you delegate the complexity of synchronization to the library itself, ensuring safe concurrent access without needing to write explicit locks or manual checks every time.
